BEARING 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
November-December 1989

North American 7-Day Event to Include Cle Elum World Cup Meet

Next summer, the world's best orienteers will converge on the small eastern Washington town of Cle Elum to take part in a World Cup orienteering meet, only the second such event ever held in the U.S. Teams from Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Switzerland, the U.S.S.R., Australia, and Canada are expected to attend the Cle Elum event, the fifth stop on the eight-meet World Cup circuit.

The World Cup meet will be held in conjunction with the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival, and will be part of the North American 7-Day Event, a two-week series of orienteering meets to be held in the western U.S. and Canada. Awards will be given for each individual event as well as for the best 5 of the 7 days of competition.

This will be a rare opportunity for you to see (and meet) the world's elite orienteers, as well as a good chance to run on some great maps. Many people from the Seattle area plan to attend the entire two weeks of events, so there should be plenty of opportunity for carpooling. Below is the entire schedule of North American 7-day events.

Aug 4-5 Canadian Orienteering Championships Kamloops, B.C. 1, 2

For more information or an entry form, contact Harvey Friedman at (206) 784-2774.

O' maps at REI

The next time you visit REI (Recreational Equipment, Inc.), you may see orienteering maps for sale. REI has purchased a selection of Puget Sound area orienteering maps to sell in their Seattle, Bellevue, and Federal Way stores.

High School League Begins 7th Season

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League will begin its 7th season with a meet on November 25 at Pt. Defiance Park in Tacoma. Nine regular meets hosted by the three Western Washington clubs will be held at various Puget Sound area parks, ending with the league Championships at St. Edward State Park in February. Anyone is welcome to attend the league meets, which are always held in conjunction with a regular club meet.

Club Meeting and Elections November 28

The November club meeting will feature officer elections, another installment of "Course Setting For Beginners," "See How They Ran the U.S. Championships," and a short talk by Mike Schuh about "How to Explain Orienteering in 5 Minutes." So, come to the University Branch of the Seattle Public Library at 50th and Roosevelt on November 28. And after the meeting, of course, will be the ever-popular gathering at Donna's Ice Cream Parlor in Roosevelt Square.
We are facing a severe shortage of candidates, so if you think you're officer material, please let Bruce McAlister (821-6862) or Eileen Breseman (522-1098) know as soon as possible. Or, just show up at the meeting. Nominations will be accepted from the floor.

Page 2

From the President

Doug Sprugel

As you can see from the income/expenses detail, the down side of holding an "A" meet is that producing a new, first-class map costs a great deal of money, which one cannot hope to recover from just one meet. The Western States Championships was no exception. The map cost a lot more to produce than we took in in registration fees. We have over 1500 copies of the map left over, which will produce income over the next decade as we hold meets on it, but for the moment, we have a severe cash flow problem. Specifically, we received a $3,000 map loan from USOF before the meet, and we are presently able to repay only $2,000 of it. We have requested an extension from USOF on the repayment and expect to receive it, but we would like to be able to pay the remainder of the loan back as soon as possible.

If you are in a position to make a donation or an interest-free loan to the club to help retire our debt, it would help a great deal. Because we are designated a 501(c)3 organization, donations are tax deductible. Any amount would be helpful. Bruce McAlister has generously loaned the club $200 to start a map fund, and has offered to put up another $300 as a matching gift, meaning that he will match any contributions we receive up to that amount. If you would like to make a contribution, please send checks to the club post office box (COC, P.O. Box 31375, Seattle, WA 98103), indicating whether it is a donation or a loan. Any contributions will be greatly appreciated.

We lost the Pig War, but who cares?

Western States Championships a Success
by Katie Sprugel

Clear blue skies, temperatures in the high 70s, views of water and islands abounding, a new map, good courses, good food, and good fun. What more could an orienteer ask?

The Western States Championships were held on September 9-10, 1989 on our new map of Moran State Park on Orcas Island in the San Juans. This was a two day, U.S. Orienteering Federation (USOF)-sanctioned class "A" meet. What that means is that there were 6 courses each day, the courses met USOF standards for distance and difficulty, and that those of you who belong to USOF accumulated ranking points by participating. This meet was held in conjunction with the British Columbia Championships held the preceding weekend on Vancouver Island. The two meets together constituted the Pig War Challenge.

We had 139 participants on Saturday (20 of them trying orienteering for the first time) and 124 participants on Sunday (14 for the first time). There were 86 people from Washington participating, 61 Canadians, 18 people from other states, and two folks far from home: Keith Curry was here from Australia in anticipation of next year's Asia-Pacific Orienteering Festival and Johann Hanssen of Sweden is here for a year as an exchange student living with the Dillons in Yelm. The local folks faced stiff competition from the Californians and Canadians, but still did pretty well. See the results in this issue for details.

Most people stayed at YMCA Camp Orkila, taking advantage of the package that co-meet director Sandy Kish Miller had arranged there. For those of you who missed it, Orkila is a superior YMCA camp. It is situated on the northwest coast of Orcas, with splendid views and sunsets over the straits. The food and amenities were great. Saturday night, Pat Albright arranged a contradance with Home Brew, a local band, and Lois, a caller from Seattle.

We are hoping that a two day meet at Moran will become an annual event. If you couldn't make it this year, start planning now so you don't have to miss all the fun next year.

[Drawing of a pig wearing boots and running]

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 16 October 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 398, Dean Coonrod, Steven Hoffman, Larry & Judy Swan, Buck Wales family
If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late.
Now on to the fun stuff. I received even less material this time than last...Don Denbo, former Seattle resident and still a member of Cascade, will be returning to the Northwest in November to accept a job at Battelle Northwest in Sequim. Don has been living in Boston for the past three years while working at Harvard University. We look forward to seeing some new maps on the Olympic Peninsula soon... Eileen & Rick Breseman will probably just be returning from a trip back East by the time you get this; they will do some orienteering in New York and New England. They did pass on a letter from Thomas Gloor, the Swiss mapper who was here for a few months in 1987. Even though he is very busy with school work, he found time to put in 500 hours to fieldcheck and draft a map in Switzerland that was used for the Swiss relay championships. He was quite pleased that the competitors thought he produced a good map. Anyway, for the next 2 years he won't accept another mapping project - so maybe school will be a little easier with all that extra time to study. He will try to come out here again next August for the full 2 week APOC90 tour and especially for our own PNWOF....Speaking of next August, I think it would be a good idea if all the club members not helping put on the World Cup and PNWOF (if not, why not?) would try to "adopt" non-local orienteers by offering to share rides and campsites and/or motels in Canada. One of the themes for PNWOF and APOC90 generally is "western hospitality," so be friendly towards everyone and you will discover a friend or two. We all want people leaving our meet with the thoughts - good map, good courses, good organization, great bunch of people.....See what happens when you all don't give me enough news to write about you- I start pontificating. O, well.... If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column, since I have such a bad memory.

Centennial Games/State Champs

Take a great new map, add interesting, enjoyable courses, mix in some nice weather, then sprinkle on some suspense. The result: a fun, exciting Washington Centennial Games.
The meet site, Riverside State Park in Spokane, is an orienteer's paradise: open, runnable pine forest with an abundance of knolls, boulders, and contour details to add interest. The courses, designed by Scott Donald of B.C.'s Kootenay Orienteers, were short but technically challenging.
To add excitement, a "chase start" was used on the second day. In a chase start, the first day's leader starts first, the person with the second-fastest time starts second by the same amount he was behind the first day, and so on. The first person to cross the finish line, then, is the winner.
The Masters Men's category provided the most excitement. Robert Miller surprised everyone (except possibly himself) by taking the lead the first day. At local meets, Miller usually walks (although very quickly, mind you), but went all out at this meet to finish the 4.1-kilometer course in 31:05, with Dave Tallent 2:06 behind, and Fred Veler only 9 seconds behind Tallent. However, an unfortunate route choice by Miller on the second day allowed both Veler and Tallent to pass him on the second half of the course. Veler had a very good run (33:00 for the 4.6-kilometer course) to take the gold medal, while Tallent got the silver, and Miller the bronze.
In the Junior Females' race, Melissa Moffett edged out Rachel Tharp by 11 seconds for the gold medal, and Shawn Perkins finished two minutes back to take the bronze.
And in the Junior Males' division, Bill MacDonald won the gold by 19 minutes over Keith Bittner, with Lee Lopez less than a minute behind Bittner.

North American 7-Day Event to Include Cle Elum World Cup Meet

Next summer, the world's best orienteers will converge on the small eastern Washington town of Cle Elum to take part in a World Cup orienteering meet, only the second such event ever held in the U.S. Teams from Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Switzerland, the U.S.S.R., Australia, and Canada are expected to attend the Cle Elum event, the fifth stop on the eight-meet World Cup circuit.

The World Cup meet will be held in conjunction with the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival, and will be part of the North American 7-Day Event, a two-week series of orienteering meets to be held in the western U.S. and Canada. Awards will be given for each individual event as well as for the best 5 of the 7 days of competition.

This will be a rare opportunity for you to see (and meet) the world's elite orienteers, as well as a good chance to run on some great maps. Many people from the Seattle area plan to attend the entire two weeks of events, so there should be plenty of opportunity for carpooling. Below is the entire schedule of North American 7-day events.
ROGAINE (Rugged Outdoor Group Activity Involving Navigation & Endurance
For more information or an entry form, contact Harvey Friedman at (206) 784-2774.

The next time you visit REI (Recreational Equipment, Inc.), you may see orienteering maps for sale. REI has purchased a selection of Puget Sound area orienteering maps to sell in their Seattle, Bellevue, and Federal Way stores.

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League will begin its 7th season with a meet on November 25 at Pt. Defiance Park in Tacoma. Nine regular meets hosted by the three Western Washington clubs will be held at various Puget Sound area parks, ending with the league Championships at St. Edward State Park in February. Anyone is welcome to attend the league meets, which are always held in conjunction with a regular club meet.

The November club meeting will feature officer elections, another installment of "Course Setting For Beginners," "See How They Ran the U.S. Championships," and a short talk by Mike Schuh about "How to Explain Orienteering in 5 Minutes." So, come to the University Branch of the Seattle Public Library at 50th and Roosevelt on November 28. And after the meeting, of course, will be the ever-popular gathering at Donna's Ice Cream Parlor in Roosevelt Square. We are facing a severe shortage of candidates, so if you think you're officer material, please let Bruce McAlister (821-6862) or Eileen Breseman (522-1098) know as soon as possible. Or, just show up at the meeting. Nominations will be accepted from the floor.

As you can see from the income/expenses detail, the down side of holding an "A" meet is that producing a new, first-class map costs a great deal of money, which one cannot hope to recover from just one meet. The Western States Championships was no exception. The map cost a lot more to produce than we took in in registration fees. We have over 1500 copies of the map left over, which will produce income over the next decade as we hold meets on it, but for the moment, we have a severe cash flow problem. Specifically, we received a $3,000 map loan from USOF before the meet, and we are presently able to repay only $2,000 of it. We have requested an extension from USOF on the repayment and expect to receive it, but we would like to be able to pay the remainder of the loan back as soon as possible.

If you are in a position to make a donation or an interest-free loan to the club to help retire our debt, it would help a great deal. Because we are designated a 501(c)3 organization, donations are tax deductible. Any amount would be helpful. Bruce McAlister has generously loaned the club $200 to start a map fund, and has offered to put up another $300 as a matching gift, meaning that he will match any contributions we receive up to that amount. If you would like to make a contribution, please send checks to the club post office box (COC, P.O. Box 31375, Seattle, WA 98103), indicating whether it is a donation or a loan. Any contributions will be greatly appreciated.

The budget for a large event such as the Western States Championships is correspondingly large. The review below is not intended to be a strict accounting, but rather an informative view of the scale of costs associated with large meets and new maps.
We lost the Pig War, but who cares?
Western States Championships a Success by Katie Sprugel
Clear blue skies, temperatures in the high 70s, views of water and islands abounding, a new map, good courses, good food, and good fun. What more could an orienteer ask?

The Western States Championships were held on September 9-10, 1989 on our new map of Moran State Park on Orcas Island in the San Juans. This was a two day, U.S. Orienteering Federation (USOF)-sanctioned class "A" meet. What that means is that there were 6 courses each day, the courses met USOF standards for distance and difficulty, and that those of you who belong to USOF accumulated ranking points by participating. This meet was held in conjunction with the British Columbia Championships held the preceding weekend on Vancouver Island. The two meets together constituted the Pig War Challenge.

We had 139 participants on Saturday (20 of them trying orienteering for the first time) and 124 participants on Sunday (14 for the first time). There were 86 people from Washington participating, 61 Canadians, 18 people from other states, and two folks far from home: Keith Curry was here from Australia in anticipation of next year's Asia-Pacific Orienteering Festival and Johann Hanssen of Sweden is here for a year as an exchange student living with the Dillons in Yelm. The local folks faced stiff competition from the Californians and Canadians, but still did pretty well. See the results in this issue for details.

Most people stayed at YMCA Camp Orkila, taking advantage of the package that co-meet director Sandy Kish Miller had arranged there. For those of you who missed it, Orkila is a superior YMCA camp. It is situated on the northwest coast of Orcas, with splendid views and sunsets over the straits. The food and amenities were great. Saturday night, Pat Albright arranged a contradance with Home Brew, a local band, and Lois, a caller from Seattle.
We are hoping that a two day meet at Moran will become an annual event. If you couldn't make it this year, start planning now so you don't have to miss all the fun next year.
[Drawing of a pig wearing boots and running]

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 16 October 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 398, Dean Coonrod, Steven Hoffman, Larry & Judy Swan, Buck Wales family If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late. Now on to the fun stuff. I received even less material this time than last...Don Denbo, former Seattle resident and still a member of Cascade, will be returning to the Northwest in November to accept a job at Battelle Northwest in Sequim. Don has been living in Boston for the past three years while working at Harvard University. We look forward to seeing some new maps on the Olympic Peninsula soon... Eileen & Rick Breseman will probably just be returning from a trip back East by the time you get this; they will do some orienteering in New York and New England. They did pass on a letter from Thomas Gloor, the Swiss mapper who was here for a few months in 1987. Even though he is very busy with school work, he found time to put in 500 hours to fieldcheck and draft a map in Switzerland that was used for the Swiss relay championships. He was quite pleased that the competitors thought he produced a good map. Anyway, for the next 2 years he won't accept another mapping project - so maybe school will be a little easier with all that extra time to study. He will try to come out here again next August for the full 2 week APOC90 tour and especially for our own PNWOF....Speaking of next August, I think it would be a good idea if all the club members not helping put on the World Cup and PNWOF (if not, why not?) would try to "adopt" non-local orienteers by offering to share rides and campsites and/or motels in Canada. One of the themes for PNWOF and APOC90 generally is "western hospitality," so be friendly towards everyone and you will discover a friend or two. We all want people leaving our meet with the thoughts - good map, good courses, good organization, great bunch of people.....See what happens when you all don't give me enough news to write about you- I start pontificating. O, well.... If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column, since I have such a bad memory.

Take a great new map, add interesting, enjoyable courses, mix in some nice weather, then sprinkle on some suspense. The result: a fun, exciting Washington Centennial Games. The meet site, Riverside State Park in Spokane, is an orienteer's paradise: open, runnable pine forest with an abundance of knolls, boulders, and contour details to add interest. The courses, designed by Scott Donald of B.C.'s Kootenay Orienteers, were short but technically challenging. To add excitement, a "chase start" was used on the second day. In a chase start, the first day's leader starts first, the person with the second-fastest time starts second by the same amount he was behind the first day, and so on. The first person to cross the finish line, then, is the winner. The Masters Men's category provided the most excitement. Robert Miller surprised everyone (except possibly himself) by taking the lead the first day. At local meets, Miller usually walks (although very quickly, mind you), but went all out at this meet to finish the 4.1-kilometer course in 31:05, with Dave Tallent 2:06 behind, and Fred Veler only 9 seconds behind Tallent. However, an unfortunate route choice by Miller on the second day allowed both Veler and Tallent to pass him on the second half of the course. Veler had a very good run (33:00 for the 4.6-kilometer course) to take the gold medal, while Tallent got the silver, and Miller the bronze. In the Junior Females' race, Melissa Moffett edged out Rachel Tharp by 11 seconds for the gold medal, and Shawn Perkins finished two minutes back to take the bronze. And in the Junior Males' division, Bill MacDonald won the gold by 19 minutes over Keith Bittner, with Lee Lopez less than a minute behind Bittner.
